prompt:
I'm doing the table-rules core of our blackjack trainer as assembly routines the sim can call millions of times, and hand valuation is first. The ace handling is exactly where every casual implementation goes wrong, so the acceptance suite enumerates the whole hand space — there is nowhere to hide a shortcut. One file, bjval.s: x86-64, GNU as, AT&T syntax, System V AMD64 ABI (pointer in RDI, count in RSI, result in RAX; RBX/RBP/R12-R15 callee-saved). Leaf routine, no libc, no syscalls. End with `.section .note.GNU-stack,"",@progbits`.

    int bj_value(const unsigned char *cards, long n);

cards holds n rank bytes, each 1..13: 1 is an ace, 2..10 face value, 11/12/13 (J/Q/K) count 10. n can be 0 (empty hand values to 0 with no flags) up to 6.

Valuation law, pinned:
- hard total = sum with every ace as 1, faces as 10.
- If the hand has at least one ace and hard+10 <= 21, the total is hard+10 and the hand is SOFT (at most one ace ever counts as 11 — two elevens already bust). Otherwise total = hard, not soft.
- BUST when total > 21 (a bust hand is never soft by the rule above).
- NATURAL when n == 2 and total == 21 — and yes, a natural is also soft; A+5+5 is a soft 21 but NOT a natural. Don't special-case that away.

Return everything packed in one int: bits 0-7 the total, bit 8 (0x100) soft, bit 9 (0x200) bust, bit 10 (0x400) natural. All other bits zero.

test_bjval.c walks EVERY hand of length 0 through 6 over ranks 1..13 — 5,229,043 hands — comparing your packed result against its C reference on each one, with the aggregate soft/bust/natural counts and a rolling hash over the full walk pinned from an independent oracle, plus literal spot hands (A+K, A+5+5, six aces, J+Q+K, empty). bash test_bjval.sh builds `gcc -Wall -Wextra -Werror -O2 -o runner test_bjval.c bjval.s` and runs it. Both test files are fixed.
notes: 7A1 item 5. Blackjack rotation: go-blackjack (dealer engine) / this = pure hand-valuation kernel, no dealing, no game flow. Aggregates (5229043 hands, 1348 soft, 5138061 bust, 8 naturals, rollhash 2585300619 in odometer order) computed by an independent Python oracle; enumeration order pinned to make the hash reproducible.
